sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Right knee pain following a bicycle fall.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports falling off a tandem bicycle with his partner approximately two weeks ago, resulting in right knee pain. He describes the injury as occurring when he hit the curb, causing immediate soreness. He took ibuprofen and applied a bandage, but the pain has persisted daily. He has been attempting to manage the pain with ibuprofen, but it does not provide adequate relief, and he read the label indicating a two-week limit. The patient reports a normal knee prior to the injury. He states that his partner was distracted and went down, and he went down as well. He has not been swimming since the injury. He expresses concern about potential surgery and a possible leg amputation.
Findings
Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation along the medial joint line of the right knee. The patient reports pain with range of motion. There is a one plus posterior drawer and a negative Lachman test. There is a negative McMurray test on both sides. The skin is intact with a dime-sized abrasion approximately one week old over the medial patella. There is no palpable crepitance. The right knee demonstrates full extension and flexion. The left knee is full and symmetric. There is no instability observed during weight-bearing. The patient reports pain with inclines, particularly uphill walking.

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Assessment
Sprained right medial collateral ligament with possible posterior cruciate ligament injury.
Plan
Order an MRI to evaluate for ligamentous injuries and meniscus damage. Prescribe a brace for immediate support and stability. Refer to physical therapy for rehabilitation and strength training. Continue ibuprofen as needed, up to three times daily. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four to six weeks to reassess symptoms and monitor progress. Consider a prescription for a less frequent analgesic if ibuprofen is insufficient.
